comments.text: EDIT: Sorry, Wrong place to hang in. It was meant as answer to VPendyala's contribution below. Congrats! This is already your second impressive result within a few days. I do not understand all details, but if a student had come into my office some years ago with such a manuscript I would have had the feeling "this guy is two classes above me". For me it would be unbelievable that a so far unknown mathematician had such a broad background and would be so fluent in writing his findings in a paper. So, again my question: Did you really come to all this by yourself, without help of AI? For a few days "we" had Fable 5, and we still have Kimi 2.7 from China, and if directed by a strong human mathematician, I would not be so surprised if they got such results. In my eyes it would be no shame if a human confessed that he got these proofs with intelligent directing of the "bots". A technical question: Do you see a chance to make the constants ($n_0$ and $c$) in your proof explicit?

Hello! Thank you very much for the kind words. Truthfully, I’ve only recently learned about arxiv, so I simultaneously posted two results I’ve been working on since four months ago. This backlog being simultaneously posted is likely why it seems unbelievable! I would be more than willing to post my some four months of notebook work showing all the failed proof routes and such if you or the community is interested. Note: I’m not sure why the 1 has been appended to my username, but it is still me, the same person. As for the technical question: sure, they could be made explicit. One would just have to trace the numerical constants through the proof: the maze geometry, Harnack-chain loss, Beurling projection estimate, Faber-polynomial bounds, and the final quantization inequality. This, by all means, would almost certainly be horrifically unoptimized and ugly, though. The reason this would look horrendous is that several steps compound constants exponentially. By the time the argument reaches the condition $n\ge e^{C_4N^2}$, even modest absolute constants from earlier estimates would produce a uselessly gigantic $n_0$ and a very small $c$.

thank you for your explanations. Some months ago (in March) I had been thinking and discussing on Erdos #1041, others as well. Perhaps you with your background and working experience would have chances to make progress there?!

I have proved Erdős’s conjecture for this problem that the extremal shortest path length tends to infinity with $n$, but not too fast. More precisely, if $S(n)$ denotes the largest possible shortest length of a path in $$ E_f={z\in\mathbb C: |z|\le 1,\ |f(z)|\le 1} $$ joining $0$ to $\partial\mathbb D$, over all monic degree-$n$ polynomials whose zeros lie in $\overline{\mathbb D}$, then for all sufficiently large $n$, $$ c\sqrt{\log n}\le S(n)\le \pi n $$ for an absolute constant $c>0$. In particular, $S(n)\to\infty$, while the sharp asymptotic order remains open. The paper for this is available as an arXiv preprint at: https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19178 A secondary paper I have written studies the length-one extreme of Erdős’s lemniscate path problem. While the main paper asks how long the shortest escape path in $E_f$ can be, this note asks when the optimal path can be a straight radius. I prove that every admissible polynomial of degree at most $3$ has such radial access, but give a certified degree-$6$ example for which every radius is blocked. Thus the first degree where Erdős’s path problem can fail to have a length-one extremal escape lies between $4$ and $6$. This secondary paper is available as an SSRN preprint at: https://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.6850818
